subject: Ellen Ammann
text: Ellen Ammann (née Sundström) was a Swedish-born Catholic social reformer and politician associated with the Bavarian People's Party, active mainly in Munich and Bavaria.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1870-07-01 in Stockholm
text: She was born on 1 July 1870 in Stockholm.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Ellen Aurora Elisabeth Morgenröte (full given names); Sundström (birth surname)
text: Authority records list her full given names as 'Ellen Aurora Elisabeth Morgenröte' and her birth surname as 'Sundström'.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Father: C. R. Sundström (professor of zoology in Stockholm); Mother: Lilly Häggström (Catholic)
text: Her father is recorded as C. R. Sundström (zoology professor in Stockholm) and her mother as Lilly Häggström (Catholic).

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: grew up in Stockholm; educated in a politically interested household
text: She grew up in Stockholm in a politically interested household and received higher girls' education.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: medical heilgymnastics (physiotherapy), beginning 1889
text: After graduating, she began studying medical 'Heilgymnastik' (today: physiotherapy) in 1889.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Dr. Ottmar Ammann (physician) in 1890
text: She married the German physician Dr. Ottmar Ammann in 1890.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1890, after marrying Dr. Ottmar Ammann, she moved from Stockholm to Munich.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: her husband's orthopaedic institute/clinic in Munich
text: She worked with her husband in his orthopaedic institute/clinic, where she learned about social problems through practice.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: Multiple sources state that she had six children.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1895, she co-founded the Marian Girls' Protection Association (Marianischer Mädchenschutzverein) in Munich, focused on protecting and supporting young women in need.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In January 1897, she helped establish the first Bahnhofmission in Germany at Munich's main station, aiming to protect girls and young women from exploitation and trafficking.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: founder/initiator and long-time leader in Munich
text: She is described as founder/initiator and long-time leader of the Catholic railway-station mission in Munich.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1904, she was involved in founding the Munich branch of the Catholic German Women's Association (KDFB) and took a leading role there.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: Quote excerpt (1904): 'Nur wer die Zeichen der Zeit gar nicht versteht ... kann die Notwendigkeit einer katholischen Frauenorganisation leugnen.'

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1909, she founded the 'Soziale und Caritative Frauenschule' in Munich to professionalize social and charitable work training for women.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Soziale und Caritative Frauenschule (Munich), precursor of today's KSH Munich
text: Institutional histories describe the 1909 school as a precursor to today's Catholic Foundation University in Munich.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: Parliamentary biography notes that she directed the social women's school until 1925.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1916: training expanded to two years
text: Institutional sources state that the training at the school was expanded to two years in 1916.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1926: state recognition
text: A diocesan biography states that the school received state recognition in 1926.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1911, she founded the Bavarian state association of the Catholic German Women's Association (KDFB Landesverband Bayern).

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: During World War I (1914–1918), she worked in war welfare/social relief and organized practical support for needy families together with other women's associations.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Luise Kiesselbach (social war relief in Munich)
text: A parliamentary biography records that she was responsible for social war relief in Munich together with Luise Kiesselbach.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1919, she founded the 'Vereinigung katholischer Diakoninnen' (later known as Ancillae Sanctae Ecclesiae), linked to the idea of a 'third vocation' for women beyond marriage or convent life.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Bayerische Volkspartei (BVP)
text: She served as a Landtag deputy for the Bavarian People's Party (BVP).

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: From 1919 until her death in 1932, she was a member of the Bavarian state parliament (Landtag).

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: women's and family support; public welfare; health; youth welfare
text: Institutional biographies describe her parliamentary priorities as welfare/public welfare, health, youth welfare, and women/family issues.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Lehrergesetz-Ausschuss; Verfassungs-Ausschuss; committee for recess period (Art. 30); Verfassungs-Ausschuss (Oct 1919)
text: In the 1919–1920 Landtag term she served on multiple committees including teacher law and constitutional committees.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: State budget committee; petitions/complaints committee; recess-period committee (deputy)
text: In the 1920–1924 term she served on committees including the state budget committee and the petitions/complaints committee.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Election review committee; petitions/complaints committee; interim/standing committee (deputy)
text: In the 1924–1928 term she served on election review and petitions/complaints committees and was deputy on interim/standing committees.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Standing committee (deputy); election review; state budget; petitions/complaints
text: In the 1928–1932 term she served on the state budget and other committees; she was also deputy on the standing committee.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In 1920, she initiated a Catholic police chaplaincy in Bavaria; sources describe this as tied to Landtag action placing a full-time Catholic chaplain alongside the state police.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1920-07-01 request for a full-time police chaplain for Munich and Nuremberg
text: A KDFB timeline records that on 1 July 1920 she requested a full-time police chaplain for Munich and Nuremberg.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In early 1923, after violent attacks by National Socialists, she joined a women's delegation that demanded the expulsion of Adolf Hitler from Bavaria from Interior Minister Franz Schweyer.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: warned of National Socialism and acted against it through 1923 and beyond
text: Institutional sources describe her as warning about and actively opposing the Nazi movement, including actions around 1923.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: During the 1923 putsch attempt, she helped endangered people escape and worked with urgently assembled government members to draft a condemnation of the attempted coup.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: helped Anita Augspurg and Lida Gustava Heymann escape Nazis during the putsch period
text: A curated biography notes she helped women activists Anita Augspurg and Lida Gustava Heymann flee from National Socialists during the 1923 putsch period.

subject: Franz Matt
text: Quote excerpt (about Ammann, 1927): '... mehr Mut bewiesen, als manche Herren ...'
evidence_note: Attributed in the nsdoku lexicon entry with a 1927 reference.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1914 Pro Ecclesia et Pontifice
text: An archdiocesan profile states she received the papal honor 'Pro Ecclesia et Pontifice' in 1914.

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: In November 1932 she collapsed after a Landtag speech about relief for large families and died shortly thereafter from a stroke.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: 1932-11-22 or 1932-11-23 (sources differ)
text: Authoritative sources differ on the exact death date: NDB gives 22 Nov 1932, while several institutional/authority records use 23 Nov 1932; one explanation is death shortly after midnight.

subject: Ellen Ammann
object: Munich Südfriedhof / Alter Südlicher Friedhof (sources describe burial there)
text: Institutional sources describe her burial at Munich's southern cemetery (often referred to as the Alter Südlicher Friedhof / Südfriedhof).

subject: Ellen Ammann
text: Summary for retrieval: Born in Stockholm (1870), she moved to Munich after marrying Dr. Ottmar Ammann (1890). She developed major Catholic women's and social-welfare initiatives (girls' protection association, Bahnhofmission, and a social-work school). From 1919 until her death in 1932 she served in the Bavarian Landtag for the BVP, focused on welfare and women/family issues, and is credited with key actions against the 1923 putsch attempt.
